content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PRACTrcps C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 c 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
November 28,2001
Allen Erenbaum
Mayer, Brown &,Platt
350 South Grand Avenue-25* Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-1503
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-242
Dear Mr. Erenbaum
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act"),l on behalf of persons now in the process of forming a
general purpose committee. Since you do not identify either the committee or the public
official you discuss, and can describe only in general terms the activities of the proposed
honorary chair, we provide you with informal assistance.'
QUESTION
Would a soon-to-be-formed general purpose committee become a "controlled
committee" under the Act if an elected city official were to serve as its honorary chair?
CONCLUSION
The answer depends on the influence wielded by the honorary chair on the
committee's decisionmaking processes. The honorary chairman's duties, as you describe
them, would not automatically require classification of the committee as "controlled."
FACTS
You represent an organization expected to become a "general purpose committee"
in the near future. This Committee will solicit funds, make contributions to (as well as
I Government Code gg 81000 - 9101.4. Commission regulations appear at Tifle 2, $$ 13109-18997, of the
California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor wilh the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation I 8329(c).)
ffiE


File No. I-01-242
Page No. 2
independent expenditures on behalf of) state and local candidates, and it will endorse
such candidates.
The proposed bylaws of the Committee provide thatitwould be governed by a
board of directors with seven voting members. The bylaws prohibit an elected official, a
candidate for elected office, orthe representative ofeither, from serving as avoting
member of the board.
The bylaws would permit the election of an honorary chairman, who would not be
a voting member of the board of directors, and would have no role in deciding which
candidates or committees the Committee would support by way of endorsement or
contribution. The Committee may ask a city elected official to become its honorary
chair. The Committee, however, does not wish to become a "controlled committee" as
defined by the Act.
The Committee would like to include the honorary chairman's name on its
letterhead, and anticipates that the chairman would provide the Committee access to his
contributor list, solicit funds in writing and over the telephone, be featured on invitations
to fundraising events, speak at such events, and distribute Committee funds in person or
by mail, even though he would have no role in deciding who would receive the funds.
ANALYSIS
"Controlled committee" is defined at $ 82016, as follows
"(a) 'Controlled committee' means a committee that is
controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate or state
measure proponent or that acts jointly with a candidate,
controlled committee, or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate
or state measure proponent controls a committee if he or
she, his or her agent, or any other committee he or she
controls has a significant influence on the actions or
decisions of the committee.
(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), a political parry
committee, as defined in Section 85205, is not a controlled
committee."
An elected official in a city or other local jurisdiction is a "candidate" under the
Act ($ 82007), and the answer to your question therefore depends on whether activities
envisioned for the honorary chairman would amount to "control" or "asignificant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee" by the elected official under
consideration for the position.
We have advised in the past that a candidate is presumed to exert a significant
influence on a committee whenever the candidate is a voting member of a committee's


File No. I-01-242
Page No. 3
leadership body. (Ferguson Advice Letter, No. A-86-044.) The Commission interprets
the definition of "controlled committee" broadly, in furtherance of the Act's fundamental
purposes. Thus, even when a candidate abstains from decisionmaking on matters directly
relatirlg to the candidate's campaign, a committee is "controlled" by the candidate if he or
she is authorized to vote on other matters. (Trimbur Advice Letter, No. 4-00-067.)
On the other hand, we have also noted that when a candidate is an "honorary"
member of a committee's governing body, one who may appear on the committee's
letterhead, but who does not vote, make decisions about committee funds, or otherwise
influence the actions of the committee, the candidate is not considered to "control" or
exert a "significant influence" on the committee. (Madden Advice Letter,No. A-85-197;
Trimbur Advice Letter, supra.)
In this case, you anticipate that the honorary chair would not be a voting member
of the board, but would further the Committee's endeavors in other ways. From your
description, it appears that the honorary chairman would be expected to lend his name
and "goodwill" to the Committee, and provide valuable fundraising assistance. As you
describe it, the role of the honorary chairman would be confined to influencing other
persons to support the Committee.
Nothing in your account of the Committee's plans suggests that the honorary
chairman would have a role in directing the Committee's activities, or influencing the
Committee's decisionmaking body. So long as this is the case, the Committee would not
be a "controlled committee" within the meaning of $ 82016. Our advice at this point is
necessarily general. If the Committee proceeds with its plan and there is any doubt
concerning the chairman's actual influence on the actions or decisions of the Committee,
or regarding the legal significance of specific activities, we suggest that you contact us
with the particulars so that we can offer you more specific advice.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
t
By. Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: Would a soon-to-be-formed general purpose committee become a "controlled
committee" under the Act if an elected city official were to serve as its honorary chair?

CONCLUSION: The answer depends on the influence wielded by the honorary chair on the
committee's decisionmaking processes. The honorary chairman's duties, as you describe
them, would not automatically require classification of the committee as "controlled."
